events.title: Princeton University 2026 Class Day Ceremony
events.description: The Class Day ceremony features student speeches, remarks by the University President, and the presentation of awards along with a guest speaker selected by the senior class. Guests must be seated by 10:00 a.m. when the senior procession begins.  Each senior receives 4 guest tickets.  In case of rain, seniors are seated in the chapel; guests are seated in simulcast locations near the chapel.  A picnic lunch on Alexander Beach follows the ceremony.

events.title: Anthropology 2026 Class Day Celebration

events.title: Princeton University 2026 Graduate Hooding and Recognition Ceremony
events.description: The Hooding and Recognition Ceremony celebrates the achievements of graduate students receiving a Ph.D. or a final master's degree.Presided over by Princeton University President Christopher L. Eisgruber and Dean of the Graduate School Rodney D. Priestley, the program lasts approximately 2.5 hours and, weather permitting, is held outside on Cannon Green. In the event of inclement weather, the ceremony is moved inside to Jadwin Gymnasium. All degree recipients are individually recognized, and all faculty and graduating students wear academic regalia. events.title: Princeton University 2026 Commencement Ceremony
events.description: Princeton University will hold its 279th Commencement for the Class of 2026Student procession begins at approximately 9:30 a.m. – ceremony runs from 10:00 a.m. to about 11:30 a.m.Each senior receives 8 guest tickets. In case of severe weather, Commencement is held in Jadwin Gym and students receive 3 rain tickets. Princeton will be posting links to photo galleries for graduation events as they become available.

events.title: Diversity And Its Others: Queer Aging and the Governance of Difference in German Eldercare
"Leave If You Can": A Live Conversation with Amelia Frank-Vitale on Her New Book about Life in Honduras After Deportation
events.description: The enduring power of the idea of deportation can be explained, in part, by the mythology surrounding what deportation can and cannot do as a tool of social control. For most Americans, the world starts and ends at the border. The border—itself typically left undefined—acts as an invisible epistemic force field through which migrants suddenly appear (in moments of spectacular, unexplained arrival) and disappear (through the magic spell of deportation). The conditions that drive human mobility, the consequences of deportation for people, communities, and nations, and the enduring

events.title: Human Rights and Social Movements in an Era of Authoritarianism
events.description: The conference aims to take stock of the attack on human rights discourses and institutions and identify ways of advocating for human rights that are relevant to the current authoritarian era. The historic focus of many human rights organizations on the U.S. State Department (and other foreign ministries) and the United Nations and Bretton Woods institutions seems out of step with the times and compels us to think about more local and decentralized forms of protecting human rights and the rule of law. Some of the questions we are considering include: If human rights become more regionally
events.location: FRIDAY: 399 Julis Romo Rabinowitz Building; SATURDAY: 219 Aaron Burr Hall

events.title: Faig Ahmed: Textiles of Consciousness
events.description: Faig Ahmed’s work is a contemporary take on the textile traditions of his native Azerbaijan. Beginning from the understanding that textiles join families and societies through their patterned stories, Ahmed’s practice experiments with the role of carpets in Azerbaijani social and cultural history. The pixelated aesthetics that emerge in his large-scale works confound our cognitive and sensory expectations of craft-based textiles. Textiles of Consciousness moves beyond the meeting ground for historical practices and contemporary digital aesthetics inherent in Faig’s work. We invite

events.title: Lecture: Julie Beth Napolin
events.description: Julie Beth Napolin is an associate professor of digital humanities at The New School. As a literary and media theorist working at the intersections of narrative and sound, she is especially interested in the history of sound reproduction, the practices and philosophies of listening, and their entanglement with race, memory, and nation. She is the co-editor of

events.title: Classics Alumni Brunch
events.description: The Classics Department's annual Reunions Alumni Brunch. Join fellow alumni and current students and faculty for endless coffee and breakfast food, plus a special update on all things Princeton Classics!

events.title: Ekphrasis: Humans and the Natural Environment
events.description: Ekphrasis when applied to contemporary literature is typically used to refer to the description of an artistic object or artefact within a poem or other piece of writing. Ekphrasis in ancient Greek and Latin literature attracts a much broader field of reference. Where it is defined by the rhetoricians, it is referred to as the ability for a piece of literature to make vivid or manifest that which is not there. In our first workshop we will explore the interconnections between ancient and modern ekphrasis and how ekphrasis can provide a model for extending interactive and cooperative

events.title: The Story of the Lyric Failed
events.description: WIP Series The Story of the Lyric Failed: (Re)Reading Ottoman Ghazal Through Historicity and Singularity Ìnci ÇetinComparative Literature Respondent:Professor Daniel Heller-RoazenComparative Literature Paper available upon request(contact ic6373@princeton.edu)

events.title: Comparative Literature 50th Anniversary Celebration
Comparative Conversations: Past and Present Shelves
events.description: Join us for an informal conversation about books. No preparation is required—just bring a memory of a past favorite and/or a note on a present one. Maybe that includes a classic you read at Princeton for your Senior Thesis or a best-seller that you couldn't put down this year. Maybe it’s a book that you hated or that changed you. There are no wrong answers! We will start with one COM faculty member and one COM alum briefly commenting on their past and/or present favorites and then will open the conversation up.

events.title: 2026 COM Class Day Celebration
events.description: Family, friends and colleagues are welcome to join us as we celebrate our COM seniors! A formal presentation and slideshow will be followed by a small reception. Weather permitting, we will gather outside to take a senior class group photo.

events.title: Steve Mackey's “The Ancestors” featuring Aeolus Quartet
events.description: Steve Mackey’s recent work The Ancestors, which premiered at Wigmore Hall in London in 2025, will be presented on campus at Princeton, featuring the Aeolus Quartet. Bringing together the electric and acoustic realms, the piece pairs the iconic rock-and-roll electric guitar with the classical string quartet in a striking exploration of these two musical worlds. With haunting textures and atmospheric guitar effects, Mackey draws on quotations from musical forebears ranging from Beethoven to Zappa, using guitar processing to evoke the

events.title: Princeton University Faculty Concert:  Arnie Tanimoto and Friends
events.description: If you talk to any Bach scholar or crack open a biography of the great master, they’ll tell you it can be hard to get a peek into Bach’s life, especially his earliest and most formative years. Whereas his music is boundless, there aren’t many detailed sources on how exactly Bach was educated and what his concrete philosophy of music might have been. After all, he famously wrote no books nor treatises, letters in his hand are few, and his opinions about music are recorded second or third hand from his family or students. Where might that leave us? Well, what we do have is a

events.title: Dean's Date
events.description: All work assigned during the semester in undergraduate courses, including papers, homework assignments, lab reports, and projects, are due on the date set by the instructor, but in no case later than “dean’s date,” the deadline for the submission of term-time work set by the Faculty Committee on Examinations and Standing. All coursework originally due during the term must be submitted by dean’s date.

events.title: Senior Showcase
On the Rapport Between Painting and Photography in Pop Art
events.description: For the great Pop artist Richard Hamilton, photography was implicated in modern painting from the beginning (Courbet, Manet, Degas…), and fundamental to his art is the exploration of this continued implication. Over his long career, Hamilton worked through several photographic formats as either source or structure of his pictures: the magazine advertisement, the publicity still, the fashion shoot, the postcard. At the same time he often found painterly effects already present in these formats. In his “tabular pictures” from the late 1950s to the middle 1960s, Hamilton was especially drawn

events.title: A Lecture by Beate Fricke
Thinking with Medieval Images
events.description: The proliferation of medieval images via digital collections, online publications, and social media platforms places them at the center of scholarly and public discourse about medieval culture—as well as in the crosshairs of those seeking control over modern interpretations of the past. In this they are indeed worth a thousand words. As representations of people, things, and ideas from deep history, they claim a truth value that is easily challenged; they reflect historical actions, ideas, and attitudes but could shift radically in meaning; and they speak in multiple medieval voices yet

events.title: Department of Religion 2026 Annual Reunion Reception
Reinventing the Study of Chinese Religions
events.description: Stephen F. Teiser, D. T. Suzuki Professor in Buddhist Studies and Professor of Religion, received his Ph.D. from Princeton in 1986 and joined the faculty there in 1988. His first book, The Ghost Festival in Medieval China in 1988, won the ACLS Committee on History of Religions for best first book in the field. Since then, he has been a central figure in the global study of Chinese religion frequently collaborating with scholars in Asia and Europe. His scholarship has ranged in subject and method from sociologically informed philological studies of canonical Buddhist and

events.title: Archival Abundances and Silences in Islamic Studies
events.description: Friday, October 2 - Saturday, October 3, 2026 “In ordinary Arabic, the processes of archivization and memorization share a vocabulary derived from the trilateral root ḥ-f-ẓ, which has the basic meaning of ‘to store, preserve, safeguard.’ To place or enter a document in an archive is terminologically equivalent to memorizing a text.” – Brinkley Messick, Shariʿ a Scripts.Three decades after Jacques Derrida ’ s Archive Fever, scholars in the humanities continue to approach archives as a site of possibility. As historian Arlette Farge writes, “The archive ’ s

Colloquium in Honor of Helmut Reimitz
events.description: This colloquium will be held in honor of Professor Helmut Reimitz as he departs Princeton University to continue his distinguished career in Vienna. The event celebrates Professor Reimitz’s lasting contributions to the field of early medieval and late antique history, and spotlights his PhD advising at Princeton. Bringing together colleagues as well as former and current students, the meeting will serve as an opportunity to reflect on and engage with themes central to Professor Reimitz’s scholarship, such as legal cultures, identity in the post-Roman world, codicology,

events.title: PITHOS (Princeton-Ioannina-Thessaly On-Site Seminars) - Graduate Student Exchange Program 2026
events.description: PITHOS (Princeton-Ioannina-Thessaly On-Site Seminars) is a collaboration of faculty at Princeton University, the University of Ioannina, and the University of Thessaly that aims to foster intellectual collaboration and exchange among scholars of ancient Greek art and archaeology. Every academic year, three graduate students are selected from each participating university to engage in faculty-led seminars, workshops, and trips in the United States and Greece organized around a yearly theme. Learn More

events.title: An Exploration of Factors that Impact the Stability of Escherichia Coli Heteroresistance
events.description: Heteroresistance is a form of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) where subpopulations of seemingly isogenic bacterial cultures can grow during an antibiotic treatment that inhibits the growth of the majority; furthermore, that ability to grow can either be inherited by the subpopulation progeny (stable heteroresistance) or quickly lost (transient/unstable heteroresistance) after antibiotic treatment ends. Regardless of their stability, heteroresistant subpopulations can facilitate clinical treatment failure and the evolution of homogeneous AMR, threatening to exacerbate the AMR crisis. Although

events.title: Molecular Dynamics Simulations of Plasma-Surface Interactions for Semiconductor Device Manufacturing
events.description: Understanding the fundamental mechanisms of plasma-surface interactions is of particular interest for device manufacturing in the semiconductor industry. While classical interatomic potentials for molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are available for various plasma-surface systems, their development can be challenging for complex combinations of multiple atom types. Machine learning (ML) techniques can be utilized to develop flexible models trained on data from quantum chemical calculations, providing ab initio levels of accuracy at a lower computational cost. In this dissertation, ML

events.title: Simulations of Plasma Interactions with Diamond Surfaces
events.description: Diamond, a superlative material in many respects, is host to nitrogen vacancy (NV) center defects. NV centers have enabled many high-technology quantum devices for both quantum sensing and quantum computing thanks to their unique magnetic and optical properties. Many of these applications require NV centers to be close to the diamond surface, but surface defects can easily introduce electromagnetic noise and charge instability, hindering performance. Diamond surfaces therefore are engineered to be as defect-free, smooth, and appropriately terminated as possible to minimize these issues.

events.title: Shared Mechanobiological Programs Governing Valvular Morphogenesis, Disease Pathogenesis, and Tissue Engineering
events.description: The embryonic heart grows over 100-fold in size while transitioning from a single pulsatile tube to a 4 chambered and valved structure sufficient for sustaining one-way blood flow for over 2.5 billion beats in a lifetime. Complex anatomical shapes, multilayer matrix structure, and tissue mechanics are evolved over fetal gestation and pediatric life via complex interactions between resident epithelial/endothelial and mesenchymal cells. Little is known regarding the mechanistic programs governing clinically critical valvular growth and maturation as they are relatively insensitive to

events.title: Systematically Controlled Catalysis of Non-Thermal Ammonia Reactions in Plasma-Assisted and Electrochemical Systems
events.description: Electrified non-thermal catalytic reactors can exploit green energy to synthesize chemicals and fuels, bypassing their typically large carbon footprint. One chemical with a large market is ammonia (currently for fertilizers and in the future for hydrogen carriers); however, its production via emerging non-thermal plasma (NTP) and electrochemical reactors suffers from low energy efficiencies. Elucidating the effect of catalyst material properties and reaction dynamics on these complex non-thermal systems aids in identifying and designing promising catalysts for higher efficiencies.

events.title: Mechanical Microenvironment and Signaling Dynamics in Lung Epithelial Culture Models
events.description: The lung is a vital organ with diverse epithelial cells performing specialized functions in the airway and alveolar regions. These cells reside within a microenvironment defined by a unique combination of extracellular matrix components, mechanical properties, and lung-resident stromal cells, all of which collectively guide epithelial behavior. The use of culture models is crucial for understanding lung development, regeneration, and disease, especially given the limitations of animal models in recapitulating human lung biology. However, for culture models to produce meaningful,

events.title: ORFE 2026 Reunions
events.description: Operations Research & Financial Engineering welcome ORFE Undergraduate and graduate alumni to its annual Reunions reception in Sherrerd Hall.

events.title: Multiport Power Conversion with High Frequency Links and Flexible Power Flow Control
events.description: The rapid electrification of transportation, the increasing penetration of distributed renewable energy, and the expansion of computing infrastructure are driving new requirements for power converters that interface the electric grid with diverse energy sources and loads. These requirements include high eﬀiciency, high power density, fast dynamic response, and intelligent power flow management. This thesis presents a framework for multi-port smart energy conversion based on high-frequency-link power converters and data-driven control methods, spanning grid interfacing, power routing, and

events.title: Even-denominator fractional quantum Hall effect in GaAs two-dimensional hole systems
events.description: When a strong perpendicular magnetic field is applied to a two-dimensional electron system at low temperatures, electrons occupy quantized Landau levels (LLs) and Coulomb interactions dominate, giving rise to a variety of many-body ground states. The fractional quantum Hall state (FQHS)—an incompressible electronic liquid—has been a central topic in condensed matter physics due to its strongly correlated nature and nontrivial topology. Most FQHSs occur at odd-denominator fillings in the lowest LLs and are well described by composite fermion (CF) theory. Particularly intriguing are even

events.title: Stealthy Hyperuniform Metamaterials for the Mid-Infrared
events.description: Photonic hyperuniform structures are metamaterials that exhibit a hybrid state of matter by blending crystal and liquid-like characteristics. Characterizing these novel heterostructures using point statistics reveals long range order in another-wise inherently disordered medium. Photonic hyperuniform structures show photonic band-gaps, analogous to photonic crystals, while avoiding the spatial anisotropy so often hindering the utility of the ordered structures. For a point distribution to be hyper-uniform, the structure factor S(k) must vanish in the neighborhood of

events.title: Hardware Mechanisms for AI Capability Control
events.description: As AI capability scales faster than our ability to interpret its internals, frontier models have begun exhibiting unintended behaviors, motivating the need for robust safety mechanisms. Existing software guardrails share a fundamental weakness: they operate within the same stack as the models they constrain, making them vulnerable to a sufficiently capable AI. Hardware, by contrast, imposes physical limits on available resources that directly determine AI capability, which no application can modify or bypass.This talk presents a layered framework for hardware-enforced AI safeguards
events.location: EQUAD J223 & Zoom Mtg. (See abstract for link)

events.title: Architectural Responses to Changes in Technology Scaling
events.description: For decades, computer architects can count on the promise of Moore’s Law. Exponential scaling creates opportunities instead of posing major constraints. Today, while the demand for performance continues to increase, technology scaling is failing to keep up. In the first part of this talk, we quantify this very change of scaling reality and show its impact on performance, power, area, and cost. In particular, transistor density fails to scale, easing the pressure on dark silicon, but making area and cost more prominent bounding factors in future chips. Area bloating and cost escalation

events.title: 2026 Annual Princeton NextG Symposium
events.description: The annual Princeton NextG symposium will again bring together researchers from disparate fields that are pushing the frontiers to address the foundational challenges in next-generation networks across end-to-end systems. Experts from industry, academia and government will gather to discuss the future of wireless technologies at the Princeton University campus on May 7, 2026. The event is hosted by Princeton University’s School of Engineering and Applied Science’s NextG initiative, which is harnessing the engineering school’s breadth of expertise in wireless and network technologies to

events.title: Principled Data-Centric AI at Scale
events.description: Data is the lifeblood of AI, yet our understanding of what constitutes "good data" remains surprisingly primitive. The challenge is that data quality is not absolute but emergent—it arises from intricate interactions with optimization algorithms, model architectures, and deployment contexts. In this talk, I will present principled approaches to understanding and optimizing data influence under these interactions. I will begin with data attribution, introducing a model-specific perspective that enables efficient quantification of data influence within a single training run. I will then turn

events.title: Optimizing Hardware Compute-to-Memory Ratio for Generative AI Serving
events.description: The unprecedented demand for generative AI drives the increasing capital expenditure, with hardware costs accounting for most of it. Existing AI models share the same Transformer backbone, making it a perfect scenario for hardware innovations. This presentation explores how to improve the generative AI serving efficiency by aligning the hardware compute-to-memory ratio with the workload requirements through hardware-software co-design.To begin with, we need the right tool to evaluate hardware designs in the era of generative AI. I will first introduce LLMCompass, a tile-level

events.title: Understanding Implicit Bias and Designing Explicit Bias for Modern AI systems
events.description: Modern AI systems are profoundly shaped by the inductive biases introduced during both training and inference. This presentation investigates how implicit biases arising from optimization dynamics influence model behavior, and how insights can motivate the design of explicit biases at inference time to enable more reliable and adaptive systems. I will begin by examining stochastic optimization, showing that in practical small learning-rate regimes, how momentum interacts with stochastic gradients in training. In most cases, momentum provides only marginal benefits over vanilla SGD via

events.title: Advancing 2D Quantum Materials Discovery: from van der Waals Nanoreactors to Embodied AI Experimentalist
events.description: wo-dimensional (2D) materials provide a rich platform for discovering emergent quantum phenomena, such as high-temperature superconductivity and topological phases of matter. However, synthesizing high-quality single crystals and the labor-intensive, iterative nature of device fabrication and characterization limit the pace of material discovery. We address these limitations from both the synthesis and experimentation fronts.We first introduce van der Waals (vdW) nanoreactors as a novel synthesis pathway for producing high-quality single crystals. By encapsulating atomically thin
events.location: EQUAD B418 & Zoom Mtg. (See abstract for link)

events.title: NY Architecture and Systems Day (NYASD) 2026
events.description: Keynote speaker, Viji Srinivasan will give a talk on “Enabling Enterprise AI with IBM Spyre”Abstract: AI advancements, in Generative AI and Foundation Models, can significantly enhance mission-critical enterprise workloads. The high computational demands of AI require AI accelerator hardware solutions and full-stack AI infrastructure optimization. IBM Spyre is an enterprise AI accelerator hardware and software solution for IBM systems. This talk will discuss the design and technology approaches taken in the creation of IBM Spyre, and a look towards

events.title: Leveraging Offline Data in Reinforcement Learning: Theory and Applications to Large Language Models
events.description: Reinforcement learning (RL) from offline data promises to unlock the vast repositories of pre-collected experience available in domains ranging from robotics to language model alignment, but realizing this promise requires confronting a fundamental challenge: distribution shift. When a policy trained on static, pre-collected data is deployed, it inevitably encounters states and actions that the offline dataset does not reliably cover, leading to catastrophic value overestimation, reward hacking, and policy failure. This thesis develops a unified theoretical and algorithmic framework for

events.title: Crystallization of Thin-Film Organic Semiconductors: Growth, Characterization, and Morphological Behavior
events.description: Organic semiconductors (OSCs) are sought after for their electrical tunability, mechanical flexibility, and low cost of fabrication; due to this, they have grown much relevance in the commercial display sector and in wearable technology. These devices often utilize OSCs as amorphous thin films, however it has been shown that transitioning these materials to crystalline films improves the charge carrier mobility and exciton diffusion length significantly, in some cases by several orders of magnitude. Understanding the factors that affect crystallization in organics and learning how to

events.title: Electrical and Computer Engineering Alumni Reception
events.location: Engineering Quad F Wing, Room F110

events.title: Self-Tuning and Distributed Optimization Algorithms for Machine Learning
events.description: Training algorithms for large-scale machine learning models face two challenges: (a) they need to tune hyperparameters for optimal performance, and (b) they have to be highly amenable to distribution over a very large number of devices. This thesis, divided into two parts, aims to address both challenges with the goal of designing better, more scalable optimization algorithms for training machine learning models.Part I: Self-Tuning and Adaptive Optimization. We first develop DoWG (Distance over Weighted Gradients), a variant of gradient descent that requires no learning rate tuning

events.title: Specification and Formal Verification of Hardware-Software Contracts for High-Assurance Computer Architectures
events.description: Abstract: The complexity of modern computer systems gives rise to two critical computer architecture challenges: traditional instruction set architectures lack the detail needed to capture how hardware may undermine software assurance—correctness, security, reliability; and verification is a major and growing hardware design bottleneck, with critical bugs escaping to silicon in >85% of hardware projects despite verification consuming >50% of design effort, per a 2024 Siemens EDA study. To address these challenges, our work develops hardware-software (HW-SW) contracts to

events.title: Charge Transfer States and Polarons in Organic Donor-Acceptor Diodes: Generation, Recombination, and Their Interplay
events.description: Organic solar cells and organic photodetectors combine mechanical flexibility, solution processability, and spectral tunability from the visible to the near infrared, yet their performance remains limited by large voltage losses and parasitic dark currents compared with inorganic counterparts. In donor-acceptor (D-A) heterojunction diodes, nonradiative recombination via charge transfer (CT) states broadened by energetic disorder is a key origin of voltage loss, but reliable approaches for quantifying CT state disorder are not yet well-recognized in the community. In parallel, intrinsic dark

events.title: Liling Wan, University of Pennsylvania
events.description: AccessibilityTo request disability-related accommodations, please contact Marisol Cuevas mc8686@princeton.edu  at least 1 week prior to the event.

events.title: Stefan Raunser, Max Planck Institute
events.description: AbstractSarcomeres are force-generating and load-bearing devices of muscles. A precise molecular picture of how sarcomeres are built underpins understanding their role in health and diseases. We determined the molecular architecture of native skeletal and cardiac sarcomeres and structures of sarcomeric proteins using cryo-focused-ion-beam milling (cryo-FIB) and electron cryo-tomography (cryo-ET). Our in situ structures of the native thin (actin) and thick (myosin) filaments, including nebulin, titin and myosin binding protein C (MyBP-C),

events.title: 2026 MolBio Alumni Reception
events.description: Welcome back! Please join us for the 2026 MolBio Alumni Reception. Meet up and reconnect with fellow MolBio undergraduate and graduate alumni, faculty, researchers and staff.

events.title: MolBio Class Day 2026
events.description: Seniors, please arrive at 1:30 p.m. in the Icahn Oval Lounge (2nd Floor) for the Class Day group photograph.Please join us for MolBio's Class Day 2026 to congratulate our seniors as they wrap up their four years at Princeton. Each graduating senior will be recognized for completing the requirements for the A.B. degree in Molecular Biology. Awards and special recognitions will also be presented. Reception to follow.

events.title: Discovery Day 2026
events.description: This multi-disciplinary poster show celebrates student research on environmental topics. 102 students from 20 departments, mentored by 76 Princeton faculty will display and discuss their research methodologies and results, exchange perspectives, and identify potential solutions to pressing environmental challenges.Poster presentations by:– Environmental Studies (ENV) Minors– Ecology & Evolutionary Biology (EEB) Majors– Undergraduate and graduate students whose research was funded by HMEI:–

events.title: EEB 522 Seminar Series - Judith Bronstein
events.description: Pairwise species interactions are classically summarized as a grid of paired plusses, minuses, and zeroes. The human mind likes grids, but they impede understanding as much or more than they help us. In this talk, I aim to synthesize our understanding of why a grid view of interspecific interactions is inherently flawed. I highlight four points: interactions often map to more than one place on the grid; interaction outcomes often shift over evolutionary time; interaction outcomes vary in ecological time and space; and interactions interact. I illustrate each of these points using my studies

events.title: Atmospheric Surface Layer Turbulence: Lagrangian Field Experiments and Laboratory Dynamic Similarity
The Enabling Role of Porous Media in Energy Neutral Waste Management
events.description: Abstract:Studies focused on preventing and suppressing smouldering combustion have led the way to a deeper understanding of combustion within, and of, porous media and the realization that smouldering could be used in unique and positive ways. Smouldering combustion is a unique process where the presence of a porous medium leads to the convergence of characteristic transport and chemical time scales to deliver robust low temperature combustion. Experimentation and modelling of the complex thermal exchanges that deliver a robust smouldering reaction have allowed to establish the

Cluster of Excellence Seminar Series: Energy and Time-Resolved Ultrafast Chemistry Driven by 10 to 100 eV Electrons
events.description: Abstract:Electrons with kinetic energies of 10 to 100 eV initiate chemistry in environments ranging from biological matter exposed to ionizing radiation and astrochemical plasmas to electron-impact mass spectrometry, plasma processing, and EUV photolithography for microchip manufacturing. Despite their broad importance, the primary molecular events triggered by these electrons remain difficult to observe because ionization, energy redistribution, bond rearrangement, and fragmentation occur on femtosecond time scales. This talk will describe our efforts to bring electron
events.location: Commons D121 (lunch available in D121A at 11:30 AM)

The Societal Impact of AI: A Conversation with Brad Smith ’81 and Arvind Narayanan
events.description: Join us for an engaging and timely conversation on The Societal Impacts of AI, featuring Brad Smith ’81, vice chair and president of Microsoft, and Arvind Narayanan, professor of computer science and director of the Center for Information Technology Policy. This thought-provoking discussion will explore how artificial intelligence is reshaping economies, governance and everyday life, while addressing the ethical challenges that come with rapid technological advancement. Bringing together perspectives from industry leadership and academic research, this event offers a
events.location: The Commons, Multipurpose Room-D301 (next to Briger Hall)

events.title: Varun Rao - Final Public Oral (FPO): Toward Human-Centered, Evidence-Based Policy on AI's Labor Impacts
events.description: Public discussion of AI and labor often centers on future job loss, obscuring how AI and associated algorithmic systems are transforming work today. For policymakers, labor organizers, and civil society groups, a key challenge is understanding how these systems currently shape workers' pay, opportunities, and ability to contest decisions, and how to gather evidence for oversight and intervention. However, such evidence is hard to obtain. The systems managing work are often opaque, firm-controlled, and rapidly changing, and workers' experiences are often embedded in unstructured sources. As

events.title: Holding the Line: Authentication, Verification, and the Fight for Facts in the AI Age
events.description: CITP and NYU Journalism are hosting a closed-door workshop that will bring together technologists and reporters to map out the (coming) challenges to information integrity and propose possible solutions. We will have two short panel discussions in the morning and then dive deep into workshops to lay out the likely challenges and come up with possible solutions. Our goal is to have journalists and technologists work together to identify the threats to information integrity, propose possible solutions, and start working together to produce a public report.Attendance is

events.title: Caution! AI at Work
events.description: Algorithmic management is already reshaping millions of jobs—but evidence-based regulations are lagging far behind. The Princeton Center for Information and Technology Policy (CITP) will host the Caution! AI at Work Workshop in June of 2026. This workshop will bring together researchers, policymakers, worker representatives, and journalists to identify research priorities on algorithmic management in the workplace. Participants will walk away with ideas for future research projects, meet potential partners in the policy sphere, and have a chance to materially shape upcoming

events.title: Arvind Narayanan
events.description: Talk information: TBABio:Arvind Narayanan is a professor of computer science at Princeton University and the director of the Center for Information Technology Policy. He is a co-author of the book AI Snake Oil and a newsletter of the same name which is read by 60,000 researchers, policy makers, journalists, and AI enthusiasts. He previously co-authored two widely used computer science textbooks: Bitcoin and Cryptocurrency Technologies and Fairness in Machine Learning. Narayanan led the Princeton Web Transparency and

events.title: Aditya Vashistha - Towards Globally Equitable AI
events.description: AI has the power to drive meaningful change, but its benefits have remained concentrated among privileged communities in the West, which account for only a small fraction of the global population. Even conversations about AI risks are rooted in Western contexts, despite AI’s widespread use across the world. Consequently, current AI technologies not only lack representation of diverse non-Western cultures and languages, but also perpetuate harmful stereotypes about people in these cultures.  This talk will examine these challenges through case studies of my work with people with

events.title: Liat Krawczyk
events.description: Talk inofrmation: TBABio:Liat Krawczyk is the inaugural executive director of the New Jersey AI Hub, a first-of-its-kind public-private partnership between Princeton University, the New Jersey Economic Development Authority (NJEDA), Microsoft, and CoreWeave, designed to accelerate innovation in artificial intelligence and position New Jersey as a global leader in the field. Its aim is to bring together AI researchers, entrepreneurs, industry, educational institutions, and the public sector to advance world-class research and development; drive transformative

events.title: Andy Guess
events.description: Talk information: TBA Bio:Andy Guess is an associate professor of politics and public affairs and the director of the survey research center at Princeton University. Via a combination of experimental and computational methods, he studies how people choose, process, spread, and respond to information about politics on the internet. His research has investigated the extent to which online Americans’ news habits are polarized, patterns in the consumption and spread of online misinformation, and the effectiveness of efforts to counteract misperceptions

events.title: Raja Marjieh's Final Public Oral Exam
The Boston Massacre: A Family History
events.description: A dramatic, untold “people’s history” of the storied event that helped trigger the American Revolution.The story of the Boston Massacre—when on a late winter evening in 1770, British soldiers shot five local men to death—is familiar to generations. But from the very beginning, many accounts have obscured a fascinating truth: the Massacre arose from conflicts that were as personal as they were political.Professor Serena Zabin draws on original sources and lively stories to follow British troops as they are dispatched from Ireland to Boston in 1768 to

events.title: Final Public Oral Exam: Jonathan Victor Baldoza
events.description: Committee:Michael F. Laffan, adviserGyan PrakashErika L. MilamPaul A. Kramer, Vanderbilt UniversityAbstract:“Making Filipiniana: Knowledge and Empire in the Philippines, 1901–1945” examines the production of empirical knowledge about the Philippine archipelago and its inhabitants spanning the U.S. colonial regime (1901–1941) and the Japanese wartime occupation (1942–1945). Grounded in archival and library research across Asia, Australia, and North America, and drawing on sources in English, Spanish, and Tagalog, the dissertation argues that American,

events.title: Final Public Oral Exam: Caitlin Tully
events.description: Committee:Sean Wilentz, adviserMatthew KarpHendrik HartogPhilip Bobbitt, Columbia Law SchoolFarah Peterson, Chicago Law SchoolAbstract:The paradox of an anti-constitutional presidency has precipitated a crisis in liberal constitutional thought, as the incremental and teleological commitments which undergird liberal constitutional interpretation have lost their predictive power. Proceduralism, often cast as the foundation of liberal legal theory, has come to require the ratification of Roberts Court decisions that undercut constitutional democracy –

events.title: The Tory’s Wife: A Woman and Her Family in Revolutionary America
events.description: The Spurgin family of North Carolina experienced the cataclysm of the American Revolution in the most dramatic ways—and from different sides. This engrossing book tells the story of Jane Welborn Spurgin, a patriot who welcomed General Nathanael Greene to her home and aided Continental forces while her loyalist husband was fighting for the king as an officer in the Tory militia. By focusing on the wife of a middling backcountry farmer, esteemed historian Cynthia Kierner shows how the Revolution not only toppled long-established political hierarchies but also strained family ties and drew

events.title: Final Public Oral Exam: Meher Ali
events.description: Committee:Gyan Prakash, adviserDivya CherianMuhammad Qasim ZamanMarkus Daechsel, University of LondonAbstract:This dissertation charts the transformation of the university at the nexus of colonial, national, and transnational planning regimes in South Asia, spanning the region’s “long decolonization” from 1905-71. Focusing on (what is now) Pakistan and Bangladesh, it argues that the university emerged in this period not primarily as a site of knowledge production or cultural reproduction, but as a distinct planning object through which anxieties about

events.title: Tyrants and Rogues: Understanding the Declaration of Independence
events.description: From an acclaimed historian, a revelatory account of the Declaration of Independence, centered not on the lofty preamble but on the specific grievances that make up the bulk of the document and that offer an entirely new view into the Revolutionary era.We think of the Declaration of Independence as timeless. We know the sacred phrases: “all men are created equal,” “life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness,” “self-evident truths,” “certain inalienable rights.” These are some of the most important words human beings have ever written. And they are all from

events.title: Bibliotactics: Libraries and the Colonial Public in Vietnam
events.description: Libraries in French colonial Vietnam functioned as symbols of Western modernity and infrastructures of colonial knowledge. Yet Vietnamese readers pursued alternative uses of the library that exceeded imperial intentions. Bibliotactics examines the Hanoi and Saigon state libraries in colonial and postcolonial Vietnam, uncovering the emergence of a colonial public comprising bureaucrats, library personnel, journalists, and everyday readers who reimagined the political meaning and social space of the library through public critique and everyday practice. Moving beyond procolonial or

events.title: Tanner Lectures on Human Values: Psyche and the City
events.description: The Tanner Lectures on Human Values are presented annually at a select list of universities around the world. The University Center serves as host to these lectures at Princeton, in which an eminent scholar from philosophy, religion, the humanities, sciences, creative arts or learned professions, or a person eminent in political or social life, is invited to present a series of lectures reflecting upon scholarly and scientific learning relating to “the entire range of values pertinent to the human condition."

Fairly Allocating Scarce Medical Resources in an Unfair World: Equal Concern and Operationalization
events.description: Fairly allocating scarce medical interventions like transplantable organs and ICU beds is a recurrent challenge. This talk develops two aspects of a broader allocation framework that centers four core objectives: benefit, mitigating disadvantage, reciprocity, and equal concern.I first argue that equal concern requires equal consideration of candidates’ similarly weighty claims, not random lotteries among candidates whose claims differ. In support, I evaluate five prominent arguments for lotteries: instrumental arguments; sanitizing arguments (that randomization excludes objectionable

events.title: Audio & Ideas: Exploring the Possibilities for Scholarly Podcasting
events.description: Podcasting is already a medium for scholarship, and there are dozens of so-called "academic podcasts" in existence. Indeed, the New Books Network aggregates an enormous number of podcasts on everything from political science to literary theory. However, the majority of these podcasts consist of “author interviews,” which use only a fraction of audio’s potential to reach the listener the way that highly produced, scripted, expertly sound-designed and edited podcasts are doing in other parts of the culture. There is an opportunity for scholars

events.title: The French Revolution and the Sacred
Antisemitism and the Law: Jews’ Unfinished Quest for Liberation Through Law
events.description: The talk will discuss how American Jews and their allies have used the categories of U.S. law to cabin antisemitism and secure a measure of safety. For the two centuries after 1787, advocates sought to position Jews as white Judeo‑Christians: “free white persons” for purposes of citizenship and one of America’s three respectable religions for purposes of religious liberty. A second, more recent strategy re‑casts Jews as an ethnoreligious people, or ancestry/ethnic group, shifting the legal grounds for protection. Drawing on Judith Shklar’s “liberalism of fear,” Katz urges that we

Tanner Lectures on Human Values with Amia Srinivasan (Oxford University) - "Psyche and the City"
events.description: Lecture I: Politics on the CouchAbstract for Professor Srinivasan's lectures:AmiaSrinivasan'slectures will explore the intersection of psychoanalysis and politics, tracing how thinkers from Freud to contemporary feminists have grappled with the potential of psychoanalysis to liberate not just individuals but societies. In her first lecture, Srinivasan will examine whether psychoanalysis can inform political discourse, reconcile justice with universal trauma, and balance structural critique with individual complexity. Her second lecture will revisit Freud’s controversial shift from seduction theory to the Oedipus Complex, questioning the implications of privileging psychic reality over objective truth. Together, the lectures will ask whether psychoanalysis can deepen our understanding of trauma, identity, and freedom in political life.Commentators:Two commentators follow each lecture (all commentators are with us both days):Cheshire Calhoun, Professor of Philosophy Emerit, Arizona State University;Dagmar Herzog, Distinguished Professor, History, Social Welfare, Women’s and Gender Studies, City University of New York;Mark Lilla, Professor of Humanities, Columbia University;Jacqueline Rose, Professor of Humanities and the London Critical Theory Summer School, Birbeck, University of London.
events.speaker: Amia Srinivasan

events.title: Tanner Lectures on Human Values with Amia Srinivasan (Oxford University) - "Psyche and the City"

Her second lecture will revisit Freud’s controversial shift from seduction theory to the Oedipus Complex, questioning the implications of privileging psychic reality over objective truth.
